nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and still freshwater habitats of the Amazon basin — quiet stream margins, floodplain lagoons, and pond-like backwaters where the surface is sheltered from wind and current; floats freely with its red dangling roots hanging into the water column

decorPreferences: calm, still water surface — sensitive to surface agitation
open-top or rim-only lid that lets leaves stay dry above the waterline
strong overhead lighting to elicit the red leaf and root coloration

goodWith: shrimp and snails (roots provide biofilm grazing surfaces)
labyrinth fish (bettas, gouramis) that use surface cover for bubble-nesting
live-bearer fry and other fish that benefit from shaded cover
avoidWith: tanks with strong surface agitation, spray bars, or persistent overhead drip
goldfish and large herbivorous cichlids that uproot or eat floating plants
high-flow planted tanks where the leaves cannot stay above the waterline

summary: Red Root Floater (Phyllanthus fluitans) is a small Amazon-basin floating plant prized for its rosette-shaped leaves that blush red under strong light and its iconic dangling crimson roots. Spreads steadily across still surfaces, providing shade and fry cover.
careNotes: Phyllanthus fluitans is the only widely-kept aquarium species in its genus and is instantly recognizable for the deep red colour of its dangling roots and, under sufficient light, its upper leaves. The plant is native to the Amazon basin (Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, Brazil, Bolivia, Venezuela, Paraguay, and northern Argentina) and is warmth-loving — it tends to languish below ~21 °C and should not be kept cold. Generally advised water parameters are 21-30 °C and pH 6.5-7.5; sources are largely silent on gH/kH, but the plant has a reputation for tolerating soft, slightly-acidic blackwater-style conditions as well as moderately hard community tanks. CO2 is not necessary; modest column fertilization (especially iron) is commonly recommended to deepen the red coloration alongside high light. Lighting is the single biggest lever on appearance: in low to moderate light the leaves stay pale green, and the trademark red develops only under strong illumination. Surface agitation is the most common failure mode in the hobby — strong spray bars, overhead returns, or splashing surface skimmers will wet the upper leaf surface and quickly rot the rosettes, and Modern Aquarium and Buce Plant both flag that this species 'will not survive in a tank with a lot of surface agitation.' Spread is steady but noticeably slower than Common Salvinia or Amazon Frogbit — periodic thinning is still required as the mat thickens, but it rarely overruns a tank as fast as those alternatives. Cross-source agreement is tight on temperature, lighting response, CO2 (not needed), growth rate (fast for a floater of this size, though slower than salvinia/frogbit), and the still-water requirement; the main gaps in coverage are hardness ranges and lifespan.
breedingNotes: Propagates almost exclusively by vegetative side-shoots in aquaria — healthy rosettes generate daughter plantlets that detach naturally and continue spreading across the surface. The species is technically capable of flowering (small ~1.5 mm flowers in groups of 2-4) and producing seeds, but seed-based propagation is not practical in the hobby and division of the floating mat is the only routine method.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Tropica search page rendered as navigation/footer only via WebFetch and did not surface a species detail page for Phyllanthus fluitans; primary source was effectively unusable, so Modern Aquarium and Buce Plant carried the husbandry data and Wikipedia confirmed taxonomy and native range.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Primary husbandry source after Tropica failed. Provided temperature 70-86 °F (21-30 °C), lighting moderate to high (higher light encourages red coloration), CO2 not necessary, growth/propagation fast via daughter plants, care difficulty easy, native range South America, and the surface-agitation warning.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Phyllanthaceae), origin (South America), lighting (low to high — leaves blood red under high light), CO2 (not necessary), growth rate (fast), pH (6.5-7.5), propagation (side shoots), placement (floating), care difficulty (easy), and the explicit note that the plant 'prefers low flow and will not survive in a tank with a lot of surface agitation.'

additional.name: Wikipedia — Phyllanthus fluitans
additional.notes: Taxonomy and native range reference: family Phyllanthaceae, order Malpighiales; native to the Amazon basin including Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, Brazil, Bolivia, Venezuela, Paraguay, and northern Argentina, with type locality on the Rio Negro. Notes air-pocket-bearing leaves that keep the plant horizontal at the surface and confirms green-to-red leaf colour shift with light intensity.
